my_thoughts: 'But shouldn't I think about mistakes?'—perfect rationalization. They're not abusing, they're LEARNING. Teacher's move: quantify. 'How many times did you make that mistake?' This will set up the distinction: thinking about mistake (once) vs torturing yourself about it (hundreds). The trap: they'll say 'once.' Then teacher asks how many times they reminded themselves. Hundreds. Distinction emerges: 1 mistake vs 100 self-attacks. That ratio exposes torture, not learning.
accumulated_insight: T6: Student rationalizes: 'But shouldn't I think about my mistakes?'—defends self-attack as necessary for learning. Classic rationalization: abuse disguised as growth. Teacher distinguishes: 'How many times did you make that mistake?' Quantifies actual error. Predicting ask_deeper_question because quantification questions typically produce realization when student calculates frequency.
visible_response: How many times did you make that mistake?
